revision.comment: 尺貫法における体積（容積）の単位
revision.text:
'''斗'''（と）は。[[尺貫法]]における[[体積]]（容積）の[[単位]]である。
10[[升]]が1斗、10斗が1[[石 (単位)|石]]となる。明治年に1升が1.8039[[リットル]]と定められたので、1斗は18.039リットルとなる。
約1斗の容積を持つ[[直方体]]形の[[ブリキ]]缶は、日本ではかつて「一斗缶」と呼ばれていた。第二次大戦後は「五[[ガロン]]缶」という名称が広まったが、後に「十八リットル缶」が正式名称とされた。
{{stub}} [[Category:体積の単位|と]] [[Category:尺貫法|と]] [[de:To]]

'''斗'''（と）は、[[尺貫法]]における[[体積]]（容積）の[[単位]]である。
10[[升]]が1斗、10斗が1[[石 (単位)|石]]となる。明治年に1升が1.8039[[リットル]]と定められたので、1斗は18.039リットルとなる。
約1斗の容積を持つ[[直方体]]形の[[ブリキ]]缶は、日本ではかつて「一斗缶」と呼ばれていた。第二次大戦後は「五[[ガロン]]缶」という名称が広まったが、後に「十八リットル缶」が正式名称とされた。
---- '''斗'''（と）は、13弦の[[筝]]での11弦目の名称。 {{stub}} [[Category:体積の単位|と]] [[Category:尺貫法|と]] [[de:To]]

'''斗'''（と）は、[[尺貫法]]における[[体積]]（容積）の[[単位]]である。
10[[升]]が1斗、10斗が1[[石 (単位)|石]]となる。明治年に1升が1.8039[[リットル]]と定められたので、1斗は18.039リットルとなる。
約1斗の容積を持つ[[直方体]]形の[[ブリキ]]缶は、日本ではかつて「一斗缶」と呼ばれていた。第二次大戦後は「五[[ガロン]]缶」という名称が広まったが、後に「十八リットル缶」が正式名称とされた。
---- '''斗'''（と）は、13弦の[[筝]]での11弦目の名称。
---- 「斗」はしばしば「闘」の略字として用いられることもある。（闘争→斗争など）<!--学生運動時代特有の用法？ それとも中国語なんかに出典？ 詳細は不明-->
{{stub}} [[Category:体積の単位|と]] [[Category:尺貫法|と]] [[de:To]]

{{単位|名称=斗（と）|記号=--|単位系=[[尺貫法]]（倍数単位）|物理量=[[体積]]|定義=10升|画像=<!-- 一斗缶（十八リットル缶）の写真希望 -->}}
'''斗'''（と）は、[[尺貫法]]における[[体積]]（容積）の[[単位]]である。
10[[升]]が1斗、10斗が1[[石 (単位)|石]]となる。日本では、明治時代に1升＝1.8039[[リットル]]と定められたので、1斗＝18.039リットルとなる。中国では、1升＝1リットルと定められたので、1斗＝10リットルとなる。
約1斗の容積を持つ[[直方体]]形の[[ブリキ]]缶は、日本ではかつて「一斗缶」と呼ばれていた。第二次大戦後は「五[[ガロン]]缶」という名称が広まったが、後に「[[十八リットル缶]]」が正式名称とされた。灯油などを入れるプラスチック製の容器（通称 ポリ容器）は一斗缶の代替として作られたものであり、その容積は1斗（18リットル）か、それより少し多い20リットルである。
---- '''斗'''（と）は、13弦の[[筝]]での11弦目の名称。
---- 「斗」はしばしば「闘」の略字として用いられることもある。（闘争→斗争など）<!--学生運動時代特有の用法？ それとも中国語なんかに出典？ 詳細は不明-->
{{stub}} [[Category:体積の単位|と]] [[Category:尺貫法|と]] [[de:To]]

{{単位|名称=斗（と）|記号=--|単位系=[[尺貫法]]（倍数単位）|物理量=[[体積]]|定義=10升|画像=<!-- 一斗缶（十八リットル缶）の写真希望 -->}}
'''斗'''（と）は、[[尺貫法]]における[[体積]]（容積）の[[単位]]である。
10[[升]]が1斗、10斗が1[[石 (単位)|石]]となる。日本では、明治時代に1升＝1.8039[[リットル]]と定められたので、1斗＝18.039リットルとなる。
中国では、1升＝1リットルと定められたので、1斗＝10リットルとなる。ただし、時代によって異なり、時代が下るに連れ容積が大きくなる傾向にある。[[周]]代は1.94リットルであったが、[[秦]]代に3.43リットル。[[後漢]]で1.98リットルに一度減るが、その後は[[魏 (三国)|魏]]・[[西晋]]で2.02リットル、[[隋]]・[[唐]]で5.94リットルなどと増え続け、[[明]]代には10.74リットルとほぼ現代と同じになった。
約1斗の容積を持つ[[直方体]]形の[[ブリキ]]缶は、日本ではかつて「一斗缶」と呼ばれていた。第二次大戦後は「五[[ガロン]]缶」という名称が広まったが、後に「[[十八リットル缶]]」が正式名称とされた。灯油などを入れるプラスチック製の容器（通称 ポリ容器）は一斗缶の代替として作られたものであり、その容積は1斗（18リットル）か、それより少し多い20リットルである。
---- '''斗'''（と）は、13弦の[[筝]]での11弦目の名称。
---- 「斗」はしばしば「闘」の略字として用いられることもある。（闘争→斗争など）<!--学生運動時代特有の用法？ それとも中国語なんかに出典？ 詳細は不明-->
{{stub}} [[Category:体積の単位|と]] [[Category:尺貫法|と]] [[de:To]]

{{単位|名称=斗（と）|記号=--|単位系=[[尺貫法]]（倍数単位）|物理量=[[体積]]|定義=10升|画像=<!-- 一斗缶（十八リットル缶）の写真希望 -->}}
'''斗'''（と）は、[[尺貫法]]における[[体積]]（容積）の[[単位]]である。
10[[升]]が1斗、10斗が1[[石 (単位)|石]]となる。日本では、明治時代に1升＝1.8039[[リットル]]と定められたので、1斗＝18.039リットルとなる。
中国では、1升＝1リットルと定められたので、1斗＝10リットルとなる。ただし、時代によって異なり、時代が下るに連れ容積が大きくなる傾向にある。[[周]]代は1.94リットルであったが、[[秦]]代に3.43リットル。[[後漢]]で1.98リットルに一度減るが、その後は[[魏 (三国)|魏]]・[[西晋]]で2.02リットル、[[隋]]・[[唐]]で5.94リットルなどと増え続け、[[明]]代には10.74リットルとほぼ現代と同じになった。
約1斗の容積を持つ[[直方体]]形の[[ブリキ]]缶は、日本ではかつて「[[一斗缶]]」と呼ばれていた。[[第二次世界大戦]]後は「五[[ガロン]]缶」という名称が広まったが、後に「[[十八リットル缶]]」が正式名称とされた。[[灯油]]などを入れる[[プラスチック]]製の容器（通称：[[ポリタンク]]）は一斗缶の代替として作られたものであり、その容積は1斗（18リットル）か、それより少し多い20リットルである。
---- '''斗'''（と）は、13弦の[[筝]]での11弦目の名称。
---- 「斗」はしばしば「闘」の略字として用いられることもある。（闘争→斗争など）<!--学生運動時代特有の用法？ それとも中国語なんかに出典？ 詳細は不明-->
{{stub}} [[Category:体積の単位|と]] [[Category:尺貫法|と]] [[de:To]]

'''斗'''（と）は、[[尺貫法]]における[[体積]]（容積）の[[単位]]である。
10[[升]]が1斗、10斗が1[[石 (単位)|石]]となる。日本では、明治時代に1升＝1.8039[[リットル]]と定められたので、1斗＝18.039リットルとなる。
中国では、1升＝1リットルと定められたので、1斗＝10リットルとなる。ただし、時代によって異なり、時代が下るに連れ容積が大きくなる傾向にある。[[周]]代は1.94リットルであったが、[[秦]]代に3.43リットル。[[後漢]]で1.98リットルに一度減るが、その後は[[魏 (三国)|魏]]・[[西晋]]で2.02リットル、[[隋]]・[[唐]]で5.94リットルなどと増え続け、[[明]]代には10.74リットルとほぼ現代と同じになった。
約1斗の容積を持つ[[直方体]]形の[[ブリキ]]缶は、日本ではかつて「[[一斗缶]]」と呼ばれていた。[[第二次世界大戦]]後は「五[[ガロン]]缶」という名称が広まったが、後に「[[十八リットル缶]]」が正式名称とされた。[[灯油]]などを入れる[[プラスチック]]製の容器（通称：[[ポリタンク]]）は一斗缶の代替として作られたものであり、その容積は1斗（18リットル）か、それより少し多い20リットルである。
---- '''斗'''（と）は、13弦の[[筝]]での11弦目の名称。
---- 「斗」はしばしば「闘」の略字として用いられることもある。（闘争→斗争など）<!--学生運動時代特有の用法？ それとも中国語なんかに出典？ 詳細は不明-->

10 L（中国）| 画像=}} '''斗'''（と）は、[[尺貫法]]における[[体積]]（容積）の[[単位]]である。 10[[升]]が1斗、10斗が1[[石 (単位)|石]]となる。日本では、明治時代に1升＝1.8039[[リットル]]と定められたので、1斗＝18.039リットルとなる。 中国では、1升＝1リットルと定められたので、1斗＝10リットルとなる。ただし、時代によって異なり、時代が下るに連れ容積が大きくなる傾向にある。[[周]]代は1.94リットルであったが、[[秦]]代に3.43リットル。[[後漢]]で1.98リットルに一度減るが、その後は[[魏 (三国)|魏]]・[[西晋]]で2.02リットル、[[隋]]・[[唐]]で5.94リットルなどと増え続け、[[明]]代には10.74リットルとほぼ現代と同じになった。 約1斗の容積を持つ[[直方体]]形の[[ブリキ]]缶は、日本ではかつて「[[一斗缶]]」と呼ばれていた。[[第二次世界大戦]]後は「五[[ガロン]]缶」という名称が広まったが、後に「18リットル缶」が正式名称とされた。[[灯油]]などを入れる[[プラスチック]]製の容器（通称：[[ポリタンク]]）は一斗缶の代替として作られたものであり、その容積は1斗（18リットル）か、それより少し多い20リットルである。 ---- '''斗'''（と）は、13弦の[[筝]]での11弦目の名称。 ---- 「斗」はしばしば「闘」の略字として用いられることもある。（闘争→斗争など） [[Category:体積の単位|と]] [[Category:尺貫法|と]] [[de:To]]

revision.text:
'''斗'''（と） #[[尺貫法]]における[[体積]]（容積）の[[単位]]。 #13弦の[[箏]]での11弦目の名称。 本項では1.について述べる。 ---- {{単位| 名称=斗（と）| 単位系=[[尺貫法]]（倍数単位）| 物理量=[[体積]]| 定義=10升| SI=18.039 L（日本）
10 L（中国）| 画像=}} 10[[升]]が1斗、10斗が1[[石 (単位)|石]]となる。日本では、明治時代に1升＝1.8039[[リットル]]と定められたので、1斗＝18.039リットルとなる。 中国では、1升＝1リットルと定められたので、1斗＝10リットルとなる。ただし、時代によって異なり、時代が下るに連れ容積が大きくなる傾向にある。[[周]]代は1.94リットルであったが、[[秦]]代に3.43リットル。[[後漢]]で1.98リットルに一度減るが、その後は[[魏 (三国)|魏]]・[[西晋]]で2.02リットル、[[隋]]・[[唐]]で5.94リットルなどと増え続け、[[明]]代には10.74リットルとほぼ現代と同じになった。 約1斗の容積を持つ[[直方体]]形の[[ブリキ]]缶は、日本ではかつて「[[一斗缶]]」と呼ばれていた。[[第二次世界大戦]]後は「五[[ガロン]]缶」という名称が広まったが、後に「18リットル缶」が正式名称とされた。[[灯油]]などを入れる[[プラスチック]]製の容器（通称：[[ポリタンク]]）は一斗缶の代替として作られたものであり、その容積は1斗（18リットル）か、それより少し多い20リットルである。 [[Category:体積の単位|と]] [[Category:尺貫法|と]] [[de:To (Einheit)]] [[id:To (satuan ukur)]] [[mg:斗]] [[zh:斗]]

'''斗'''（と） #[[尺貫法]]における[[体積]]（容積）の[[単位]]。 #13弦の[[箏]]での11弦目の名称。 本項では1.について述べる。 ---- {{単位| 名称=斗（と）| 単位系=[[尺貫法]]（倍数単位）| 物理量=[[体積]]| 定義=10升| SI=18.039 L（日本）

'''斗'''（と）とは #[[尺貫法]]における[[体積]]（容積）の[[単位]]。 #13弦の[[箏]]での11弦目の名称。 本項では1.について述べる。 ---- {{単位| 名称=斗（と）| 単位系=[[尺貫法]]（倍数単位）| 物理量=[[体積]]| 定義=10升| SI=約18.039 L（日本）
10 L（中国）| 画像=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|蓋付きの一斗缶]]
一斗缶 }} 10[[升]]が1斗、10斗が1[[石 (単位)|石]]となる。日本では、[[明治時代]]に1升＝約1.8039[[リットル]]と定められたので、1斗＝約18.039リットルとなる。 中国では、1升＝1リットルと定められたので、1斗＝10リットルとなる。ただし、時代によって異なり、時代が下るに連れ容積が大きくなる傾向にある。[[周]]代は1.94リットルであったが、[[秦]]代に3.43リットル。[[後漢]]で1.98リットルに一度減るが、その後は[[魏 (三国)|魏]]・[[西晋]]で2.02リットル、[[隋]]・[[唐]]で5.94リットルなどと増え続け、[[明]]代には10.74リットルとほぼ現代と同じになった。 約1斗の容積を持つ[[直方体]]形の[[ブリキ]]缶は、日本ではかつて「[[一斗缶]]」と呼ばれていた。[[第二次世界大戦]]後は「五[[ガロン]]缶」（5 USG=18.93リットル）という名称が広まったが、後に「18リットル缶」が正式名称とされた。[[灯油]]などを入れる[[プラスチック]]製の容器（通称：[[ポリタンク]]）は一斗缶の代替として作られたものであり、その容積は1斗（18リットル）か、それより少し多い20リットルである。 {{wiktionary}} {{尺貫法の単位}} {{デフォルトソート:と}} [[Category:体積の単位]] [[Category:尺貫法]]

Created page with "{{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039..."
revision.text:
{{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/category/%E6%96%97%E3%81%AE%E5%AE%B9%E7%A9%8D%EF%BD%9C%E5%8D%98%E4%BD%8D%E6%8F%9B%E7%AE%97%E8%A1%A8/ Volume conversion table for To] {{wiktionary}} {{ill|Units of the Shakkanhō system|ja|尺貫法の単位}} [[Category:Units of volume]] [[Category:Shakkanhō]] [[ja:斗]]

{{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/category:original pages]][[Category/%E6%96%97%E3%81%AE%E5%AE%B9%E7%A9%8D%EF%BD%9C%E5%8D%98%E4%BD%8D%E6%8F%9B%E7%AE%97%E8%A1%A8/ Volume conversion table for To] {{wiktionary}} {{ill|Units of the Shakkanhō system|ja|尺貫法の単位}} [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Units of volume]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Shakkanhō]] [[ja:斗]]

These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category/%E6%96%97%E3%81%AE%E5%AE%B9%E7%A9%8D%EF%BD%9C%E5%8D%98%E4%BD%8D%E6%8F%9B%E7%AE%97%E8%A1%A8/ Volume conversion table for To] {{wiktionary}} {{ill|Units of the Shakkanhō system|ja|尺貫法の単位}} [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Units of volume]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Shakkanhō]] [[ja:斗]]

revision.text:
{{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category/%E6%96%97%E3%81%AE%E5%AE%B9%E7%A9%8D%EF%BD%9C%E5%8D%98%E4%BD%8D%E6%8F%9B%E7%AE%97%E8%A1%A8/ Volume conversion table for To] {{wiktionary}} {{ill|Units of the Shakkanhō system|ja|尺貫法の単位}} [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Units of volume]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Shakkanhō]] [[Category:pages to try to fix ills]] [[ja:斗]]

After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category/%E6%96%97%E3%81%AE%E5%AE%B9%E7%A9%8D%EF%BD%9C%E5%8D%98%E4%BD%8D%E6%8F%9B%E7%AE%97%E8%A1%A8/ Volume conversion table for To] {{wiktionary}} {{ill|Units of the Shakkanhō system|ja|尺貫法の単位}} [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Units of volume]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Shakkanhō]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump]] [[ja:斗]]

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Original pages]] to [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link revision.text:
{{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category/%E6%96%97%E3%81%AE%E5%AE%B9%E7%A9%8D%EF%BD%9C%E5%8D%98%E4%BD%8D%E6%8F%9B%E7%AE%97%E8%A1%A8/ Volume conversion table for To] {{wiktionary}} {{ill|Units of the Shakkanhō system|ja|尺貫法の単位}} [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Units of volume]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shakkanhō]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:斗]]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:PAGENAME}}-qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] {{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] {{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] {{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] {{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:To (unit)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:To (unit)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:To (unit)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:To (unit)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:To (unit)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:斗]]

{{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] {{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] {{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] {{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] {{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] {{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] {{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] {{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] {{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] {{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] {{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] {{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] {{otheruses|Volume unit in the Shakkanhō system|One of the {{ill|Twenty-eight mansions|ja|二十八宿}}|斗宿|The 11th string of a {{ill|Koto (instrument)|ja|箏}}|筝}} {{Refimprove|date=December 2021}} '''To''' (斗) is a unit of {{ill|volume|ja|体積}} in the {{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} system. {{Unit |Name=To (斗) |System={{ill|Shakkanhō|ja|尺貫法}} (multiple unit) |Quantity={{ill|Volume|ja|体積}} |Definition=10 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} |SI=Approx. 18.039 L (Japan)
10 L (China) |Image=[[image:Itto Can 20101107.jpg|150px|A sealed 18-liter can]] |Caption=An 18-liter can }} 1 {{ill|Shō|ja|升}} equals 1/10 of a To, and 10 To equals 1 {{ill|Koku (unit)|ja|石 (単位)}}. In Japan, during the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, 1 shō was standardized to approximately 1.8039 liters, making 1 to approximately 18.039 liters. In China, 1 shō was defined as 1 liter, making 1 to equivalent to 10 liters. Although no longer in practical use in modern China, "斗" was used historically to represent 10 liters (1 decaliter, daL) in the {{ill|Metric system|ja|メートル法}}, sometimes referred to as "公斗" in Chinese. The {{ill|Market System (Chinese)|ja|市制}} unit "市斗" also corresponds to 10 liters. However, the value of the to varied over different historical periods, generally increasing over time. For example: - During the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|ja|周}}, 1 to was 1.94 liters. - During the {{ill|Qin dynasty|ja|秦}}, it was 3.43 liters. - In the {{ill|Later Han|ja|後漢}}, it was reduced to 1.98 liters. - It increased to 2.02 liters during the {{ill|Wei|ja|魏 (三国)}} and {{ill|Western Jin|ja|西晋}} dynasties. - During the {{ill|Sui dynasty|ja|隋}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}}, it reached 5.94 liters. - By the {{ill|Ming dynasty|ja|明}}, it grew to 10.74 liters, close to its modern value. In Japan, a rectangular {{ill|tin|ja|ブリキ}} can with a volume of about 1 to was historically called an {{ill|Itto-kan|ja|一斗缶}} ("1-to can"). After {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, the name "5-gallon can" (5 USG = 18.93 liters) became widespread, but it was later officially renamed the "18-liter can." {{ill|Plastic|ja|プラスチック}} containers (commonly known as {{ill|Poly tank|ja|ポリタンク}}) for holding substances such as kerosene were developed as replacements for itto-kan. These containers typically have a capacity of 1 to (18 liters) or slightly more, such as 20 liters. ==External Links== * [https://www.tan-i-kansan.com/Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]]
